Basically, you can parse the HTTP request buffer as you do now in order to compose the `Entry` object, but the different would be that instead of `Entry` you should compose `pook.Request`, which is the required known interface used during the mock matching phase.

From my perspective, the only useful part of `mocket` from `pook` perspective, would be the `socket` patch logic and HTTP request/response parser/builder.

I would considering giving it a try, and if you think it's introduces too much overhead on your side, we can workaround that with another approach.

The alternative I'm thinking could be optionally allowing the mock engine overwrites the mock constructor function at `pook` level, so you can do some sort of proxy/delegator pattern that would give you the ability to intercept HTTP mocks definitions via `pook` and build the required `Entry` instance at `mocket` level. That being said, I'm not quite happy with that approach and I think it would introduce overhead on your side anyway, and an unnecessary error-prone scenario.

Ok, so I re-read your whole patch and I apologize: I misunderstood your issue. I missed the fact that what Cinder is doing is testing retrying/tenacity itself and checking that `time.sleep` is being called. I had the impression it was because of eventlet (which is a common issue…) but it's actually not related – my bad. I should have dig into your patch a bit more lol.

So, the general issue here is that Cinder is testing retrying/tenacity, which is utterly useless and stupid (yeah I said it lol). I'd suggest to just delete the entire class of test, or move them to tenacity if you/they think the coverage in tenacity is not good enough. I imagine they had test before using retrying and that it was just ported with the test along. Now this is just technical debt.

Now if you really want to test that and see if `time.sleep` have been called using mock (sighs), I'd suggesting just using a custom sleep function as an argument to `tenacity.Retrying` or using the trick you suggested with `mocked_retry_sleep = mock.patch.object(time, 'sleep')`.

But as I said, I'd pick option 1 and just not try to test what is already tested elsewhere. :)

Mopidy uses Pykka to pass calls and results between actors/threads. When a call ends in an exception, Pykka sends it back to the caller and reraises it, thus it becomes part of most exception traces in Mopidy, without being at fault.

The first traceback seems to come from Mopidy-Youtube, and should be filed there, preferably with the exception itself too, and not just the traceback.

The second traceback seems to come from Mopidy itself, which tries to send messages to an `MpdSession` instance that has exited. Please open an issue in the Mopidy project with steps on how to reproduce the issue.

I don't see the current changes as anything other than very minor improvements to the current `run()` method.   `run()` already preserved the state of any existing daemon tasks.  I've merely tweaked it so that calling it with no coroutine makes it go through a single scheduling cycle.  The optional timeout argument allows you to specify/override the maximum waiting time on the internal `select()` call.   The experimental `run_coro()` currently passes all unit tests for the normal `run()` method.

The only additional change I'm thinking about is the `run(coro, timeout=seconds)` option.  That's an interesting option.  My inclination on that is to make it equivalent to `run(timeout_after(seconds, coro))`.
Although the `Kernel.run()` method is a bit fat, I also consider it to be fairly performance critical. All things equal, I'd rather it be fast than overly abstracted.

One general concern on nesting is just what happens when you go beyond 2 levels.   The two-exception idea definitely gets crazy then.

The idea of making the timeout error subclass CancelledError seems reasonable though.  That could probably work.  Let me sleep on it.

Another reason this should be treated specially is that pantable and pantable2csv are designed to be "invertible" (or strictly speaking, idempotent as far as pandoc's being idempotent). If you have a subcells filter then by definition it is not invertible.

I now have a better idea of how to group this: under an "extension" key, and add a module with name extension, where all such functions resides in. These functions would then somehow manipulate the CSV is a certain way (i.e. these functions are filters of the CSV). In this way, the regex and the "column-selection" filter should be 2 separate extension/filter functions.

Refactoring of the original code is necessary though. It was originally designed as a single file filter. But it becomes more sophisticated that a better organization is necessary.

Hi @westeve! ZAP by default does not allow you to connect to its API from addresses other than localhost (and `http:/zap`) by default. So, you also need to add the IP you want to connect to ZAP from to the allowed addresses. You can do this manually in the GUI by going to "Tools -> Options -> API" and adding the IP to "Addresses permitted to use the API". Or you can also do it in the start options. So to use the IP `172.25.0.6` as in your example, you could start ZAP like this:
```
$ zap-cli --zap-url 'http://172.25.0.6' -v start -o '-host 172.25.0.6 -config api.addrs.addr.name=172.25.0.6'
```

Though the `--zap-url` param is unneeded if the `ZAP_URL` environment variable is set. This will then allow you to connect to the ZAP API from `172.25.0.6` rather than localhost. If you're in a trusted/isolated environment, you can also let all IPs use ZAP with the following start options `-config api.addrs.addr.name=.* -config api.addrs.addr.regex=true`.

I hope that helps!

recent python that use the SSLContext object: ciphers are added to the context
older python that don't use the SSLContext object: ciphers are set in the wrap_socket() method

The only way to reach that abort() statement is if multiple fatal errors have been detected (e.g. out of bounds array accesses).  Moreover, as the message in the code points out, the only way that can happen is if the containing application is ignoring the errors and continuing to execute code which has gotten itself into an invalid state.

Think about it like this, would you disable memory segmentation so that code which runs outside the bounds of an array doesn't result in an application terminating segmentation fault?  No, you wouldn't (or hopefully you don't).  Forcing the application to continue running after it's gotten itself into an undefined state is not a useful thing to do.  What will happen is that it will crash somewhere else, or worse, continue running but give the wrong outputs.  In either case it will be very hard to debug.  If you are really unlucky the underlying error will go unnoticed for a long time, only surfacing late in the development process when it is more costly to fix.  

So no, I'm not silencing the fatal error output.

# Root Cause
I've been looking into this issue and it seems that the URI length limit is not defined in the API server software. Rather, I believe that the limit is imposed by the Apache web server itself. It [seems](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1289585/what-is-apaches-maximum-url-length#1289611) that the length of the HTTP request line is the limiting factor. And Apache limits it to [8190 bytes by default](https://httpd.apache.org/docs/2.2/mod/core.html#limitrequestline).

This is the default value, which has not been set specifically on the servers. (If we wanted to pursue having the value set explicitly on the servers rather than relying on the default, I believe [this Chef file](https://github.com/openstreetmap/chef/blob/4670fb28130f0c5f6371ddc4932bf856725ee589/roles/web-frontend.rb) would be the location to do it).

## Experimental Verification
The following code shows that a request line of 8190 bytes gives the expected result whereas a request line of 8195 bytes causes the 414 error we are addressing in this issue:
```python
len('GET /api/0.6/waysways=') + len(','.join([str(x) for x in range(1, 1854)])) + len(' HTTP/1.1\r\n')  # 8190
len('GET /api/0.6/waysways=') + len(','.join([str(x) for x in range(1, 1855)])) + len(' HTTP/1.1\r\n')  # 8195

api.WaysGet(range(1,1854))  # 404 error - expected
api.WaysGet(range(1,1855))  # 414 error - not expected
```

# Possible Solutions
Here are some of the most likely solutions.

* *Hardcode a URI length limit constant:* From an efficiency perspective, we may want to place as many Way IDs into each request as possible (and consequently use fewer HTTP requests). The downside is that any shortening of the limit would cause the 414 errors to return (although this could be easily fixed again by changing the constant). Also, it seems unlikely to me that this limit would be changed.
* *Push for a URI limit in the specification:* The OSM wiki (which may or may not be authoritative) [does not specify a URI limit](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/API_v0.6#Multi_fetch:_GET_.2Fapi.2F0.6.2F.5Bnodes.7Cways.7Crelations.5D.3F.23parameters), but instead includes a question, possibly indicating a need for future improvement: "How long is too long?" Putting the limit in the specification and in the Chef files for the Apache configuration might be the most responsible way forward and it would help any future API users understand the limitations better. This would allow us to hardcode a URI length limit with more confidence that it will not change in the future.
* *Use conservative limits to preempt future changes:* We might consider artificially lowering our length limit constant to prevent future problems that may arise if the length limit is lowered on the servers.
* *Include a constant number of IDs in each request:* We could send a constant number of IDs in each request and choose that constant such that we do not violate the length limit. A benefit of this approach is that developers can easily estimate the number of API requests will be made for each call to `NodesGet()`/`WaysGet()`/`RelationsGet()`. A potential issue is support for [64-bit IDs](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/64-bit_Identifiers) which would limit us to ~380 IDs under the current length limits. Furthermore, we lose efficiency by creating extra API calls in situations where we may not need to.

# Discussion
I'm personally leaning towards hardcoding a URI length limit constant, with or without trying to standardize the limit. I believe that the efficiency gains of this approach may be significant. Furthermore, I do not think it is likely that the length limit will be decreased in the future.

I'm interested in hearing your thoughts or alternate solutions.

This decision may be out of our hands, since `cfitsio` sets the keyclass of cards that start with `CONTINUE` to `TYP_CONT_KEY` regardless of whether or not they have an `'='`:
https://github.com/esheldon/fitsio/blob/f6482556354506b632ce8d1377ba84382db3b2b5/cfitsio3370patch/fitscore.c#L8099-L8100

In light of this, I'm re-proposing the change in 1569a44 to treat all cards that start with `CONTINU` as `CONTINUE` keys regardless of whether they have a space. The downside is that it will overwrite `CONTINUE= ` with `CONTINUE ` when a header is read and written (this is the behavior for `COMMENT =` and `HISTORY =` corrupted keys as well).

To make things more complicated, these three keys will evaluate to different values on a read and write:
```
CONTINUE  'key'   -> CONTINUE  'key' # two spaces in 9-10 place (fits convention)
CONTINUE= 'key'  -> CONTINUE 'key' # one space in 9 (not convention)
CONTINUE = 'key' -> CONTINUE = 'key # '=' in 10 with spaces in 9 & 11
```
I'm not sure exactly what is going on in the second one, but I'm guessing it is stripping the `'='` and not padding out the value.

The current behavior mirrors what happens with tables if you write in
place: the table is expanded but never contracted.  that is a feature
of cfitsio table writes.  So I did this to be consistent.

    $ ifconfig tun0

            inet ...
            inet6 ...  prefixlen 64  scopeid 0x20<link>
    $

[NLPIR的手册](http://ictclas.nlpir.org/newsdownloads?DocId=389)说：
```
用户词典需要注意的事项还包括:
1. 如果用户词有空格,需要采用[]括起来,例如: [Bill Clinton] nrf
2. 如果需要该用户词作为文章的关键词输出,必须用户词性标注为:key,如:科学发展观 key
3. 如果将一个词是人名,同时又希望作为关键词输出,则需要标注为 key_nr,如 钟南山 key_nr
4. 如果将一个词是地名,同时又希望作为关键词输出,则需要标注为 key_ns,如 钓鱼岛 key_ns
5. 如果将一个词是机构名,同时又希望作为关键词输出,则需要标注为 key_nr,如 国安 委 key_nt
```

The manual also has some more information.

Note: For those who read this.

Support for macOS is possible (iTerm2 only), the only issue is that I can't get a macOS machine I can test on. What I need to test is iTerm2's proprietary escape sequences. 

iTerm2 supports a similar sequence to the Linux terminals to change colors but it works differently and the reference page in the docs isn't very clear. 

Here's the page (Scroll down to "Change the color palette"): 

> https://www.iterm2.com/documentation-escape-codes.html

> Change the color palette
>
> `^[]Pnrrggbb^[\`
>
> Replace "n" with:
>
>    `0-f (hex) = ansi color`
    `g = foreground`
    `h = background`
    `i = bold color`
    `j = selection color`
    `k = selected text color`
   ` l = cursor`
    `m = cursor text`
>
> rr, gg, bb are 2-digit hex value (for example, "ff"). Example in bash that changes the foreground color blue:
>
> `echo -e "\033]Pg4040ff\033\\"`

Basically what we need to figure out is the exact sequences we need to modify `background`, `foreground`, `cursor` and colors `0-15`. If anyone with a macOS system would like to test I'd be grateful as I can't find these sequences in use anywhere as a reference.

More info:

- https://gitlab.com/gnachman/iterm2/issues/454

Sorry to reopen this but from what I can tell this is actually quite straightforward (few extra steps required). If you simply add your own model:
```
class ModelPlaceholder(models.Model):
    id = models.UUIDField(primary_key=True, default=uuid.uuid4, editable=False)
    default_text = models.TextField(default="Default text")
```
Then on your own model instead of using charfield etc you use:
```
editable_text_field = models.OneToOneField(ModelPlaceholder, null=True)
```
When you create an instance you just create ModelPlaceholder instance first and use it:
```
m = ModelPlaceholder.objects.create(default_text="This is what will show up for default text!")
YourModel.objects.create(editable_text_field=m)
```
Then finally in your templates you would use:
```
{% front_edit "{{ object.editable_text_field.id }}" %}
   {{ object.editable_text_field.default_text }}
{% end_front_edit %}
```

Works well! Of course its some extra work to start retrieving the actual text values using key_for on the id field etc but it should be possible. Could probably just create another field on the ModelPlaceholder thats a foreignkey to the Placeholder model and set up some signals to fill it in after first save.

The root cause found.

To operate in a netns the library starts a child process upon `NetNS()` instantiation. When the upstart tries to stop the script, it kills not the parent process, but the child, and the parent waits forever.

To be fixed today, stay tuned.
Looks like fixed.

I'm not sure it's a regression in the library.

The issue is that when you create a macvlan interface on a macvlan interface, it's being created on the master device instead, so the link to the parent points in your example to the dummy interface, not mv1.

In 0.4.18 the library simply didn't check that, so it was ok to create an interface with link to A and get it with link to B. But now it's more strict so the code above fails.

To solve the issue you simply have to create macvlan interfaces on the master device, where they're actually created, so in your example — both mv should be created on the dummy interface.

When you set `api_version` the client will not attempt to probe brokers for version information. So it is the probe operation that is failing. One large difference between the version probe connections and the general connections is that the former only attempts to connect on a single interface per connection (per broker), where as the latter -- general operation -- will cycle through all interfaces continually until a connection succeeds. #1411 fixes this by switching the version probe logic to attempt a connection on all found interfaces.

The lens catalogs shouldn't be large.  

If you're making a new NGCorrelation object each time, that might also be the issue.  Particularly if you use different min_sep/max_sep values (or in general different binning choices).  Then is might need to make a new tree for the source objects (which is where all the memory usage is) for each different lens.

The catalogs cache any trees they build, since typical use cases can access the trees multiple times, so this saves the build step from being called repeatedly (however, cf. Issue #53).  If you are asking it to make different trees each time (because of different binning options), then this could be what is blowing up your memory use.

So I think the solution is either to use the same binning for all lenses (which may not be convenient for your use case) or to manually clear the cache each time
```
source_catalog.gfields.clear()
```

BTW, the proposal in Issue #53 would be to swap the default behavior to **not** cache, but let this be an optimization that users could turn on if it is helpful for their particular use case.  It sounds like your use case is another vote in preference for this change.

It's easy from inside python-pptx. The other way is with opc-diag.

From python-pptx you just get the lxml element for the object in question and then use its `.xml` property. So say from an axis object:

```python
print axis._element.xml
```

Using [opc-diag](http://opc-diag.readthedocs.io/en/latest/install.html) would be something like this:

```bash
opc browse my-test-file.pptx chart1.xml
```

But I think in the end you'll find here that `python-pptx` is setting the XML in the intended way, just that PowerPoint interprets the XML in an unexpected way in this case.

But if you can make one by hand that looks the way you want, one can often discover the hidden mechanism at work by comparing the XML of the python-pptx generated .pptx and the one built by hand.

>what is the desired way from the project's standpoint

Any modules will do if the users finds them useful... I think is the goal.

>should all of these simple modules be implemented as new separate modules, or should external_script and the magic of format be promoted? Is there any kind of "complexity" bar for the new modules?

We're catering to everybody including average users that might not know how to read or write scripts... so they could just use modules instead... and also, technically we could have just one `external_script` module with bunch of scripts... but is much nicer for everybody to see what modules they can use... instead of having to figure out scripts themselves.

What simple modules are we talking about? I think by making modules, we can share this with others... If we make scripts, we might keep it ourselves and not sharing... because it's not perfect or is too simple.... I dunno.

>Is there any kind of "complexity" bar for the new modules?

I think the problem is translating from old formats to new formats...  Might be too hard to deprecate... and that users wouldn't understand this new stuffs... And still no documentation too... If we put out new modules with new formats, then there would be almost nothing to fix if we make all placeholders (including control placeholders) available right away for users to tweak with.

I'm a fan of including examples so users can kinda see how it works... and so they can copy and paste the configs in without doing any (or very little) adjustment.

1. CustomSparseScaler is inspired by scikit-learn's [RobustScaler](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.preprocessing.RobustScaler.html). The goal is to find the 5th percentile value and the 95th percentile value for that column. We set these as 0 and 1, and then scale all values to fit between them. I wrote my own version to be compatible with sparse data, since scikit-learn's doesn't take sparse data by default. 
2. This function just figures out the list of columns (attribute names in current implementation) in our dataset. It ignores everything in column_descriptions (which is either categorical data which can't be scaled, output column, a column marked ignore, or the y values to train a subpredictor on). 
3. AddPredictedFeature can be deprecated for now. In it's current form it is used to run clustering on our input rows, and add the prediction for which cluster this row falls into as a feature. It's not super useful, and the code isn't great. 
4. One of the most powerful things auto_ml does is automatically train and ensemble subpredictors for you on sub-portions of your problem. Once we've trained up all our subpredictors, we want to run each row through the subpredictors to get predictions from them. AddSubpredictorPredictions is where we pass each row into each subpredictor to get it's prediction.

In your first code:

```python
with token.open(user_pin=pin) as session:
    # Here is where you leave the session with a PKCS#11 object, which will fail.
    # Objects are only valid in the session they were created.
    # Objects from other session will get you an InvalidHandle, or worse, the wrong object!
    return session.get_objects({Attribute.CLASS: ObjectClass.PUBLIC_KEY})
```

In your second code:

```python
with token.open(user_pin=pin) as session:
    for i in session.get_objects({Attribute.CLASS: ObjectClass.PUBLIC_KEY}):
        # Here you are printing an object inside the session while it is still valid
        # This will work.
        print(i)
        return ''
```

In the third code:

```python
with token.open(user_pin=pin) as session:
    for i in session.get_objects({Attribute.CLASS: ObjectClass.PUBLIC_KEY}):
        # This will work because you're extracting the key from the object inside the session,
        # while it is still valid, you're then returning string, not a key object.
        # If you want PEM, this is the best option.
        return pem.armor('RSA PUBLIC KEY', encode_rsa_public_key(i)).decode()
```

To clarify: `with` starts a block that holds open the resource *only while you are inside the block*. If you leave the block due to `return`, `Exception` or reaching the end, the resource will be closed. These are called *context managers*. You will see them used for files, transactions, devices, and other resources you need to explicitly close. For this reason they are extremely useful because they prevent you from leaking resources.

You can, if you prefer, call `token.open()` directly, but you must match this with a `session.close()`, else you will leak the session (including any possible exception handling). Context managers are better, because they're easier to get right.

~

One more thing, be aware of exchanges between `str` and `bytes`. In general I try to minimise my exchanges, because eventually I'll break my unicode handling. It's better to only do `encode`/`decode` on input or output. When you do use `encode` and `decode` be explicit what you're encoding/decoding from (e.g. `utf-8`, `ascii`).

So when you capture the token serial, `encode` immediately based on the terminal/file encoding (Python has utilties for this). PEM is `bytes`, and while it is "ascii clean", unless you need to output it to a terminal, just keep it as `bytes`. The next library is probably going to consume it as `bytes`. Moving it in and out of unicode is just an opportunity for mistakes.

```python
# _*_ coding: utf-8

# To write Unicode text in UTF-8 to a xlsxwriter file in Python 2:
#
# 1. Encode the file as UTF-8.
# 2. Include the "coding" directive at the start of the file.
# 3. Use u'' to indicate a Unicode string.

import xlsxwriter

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('demo.xlsx')
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

pound_format = workbook.add_format({'num_format': u'£0.00'})

worksheet.write(0, 0, 10.99, pound_format)

workbook.close()

```

The "twelve-factor app" methodology specifically states config should be set as environment variables: https://12factor.net/config

That would be much messier with config files.

Hi @Basiczombie, thx for the feedback. As you commented the JQuery include is inside a {% block %} exactly for the reason you mentioned: most site already have JQuery included.
This plugin leverage the Django feature that is to redefine template in your app so that you can customize third party plugins.
So the way to proceed is to create in *your main application* a template/tellme/js_inc.html. I think you can  either copy paste the original js_inc.html and remove the jquery block. Some more detailed information is avialble here https://code.djangoproject.com/wiki/ExtendingAdminTemplates and there: https://code.djangoproject.com/wiki/ExtendingTemplates

Let me know if that works for you, I'll then close that ticket if that is OK.

Note: the initial reason of letting a jquery include in the default template is to simplify and reduce steps to make a first test of the plugin (so you do not have to pull multiple part from different location): a turnkey solution.

I asked the PlexTogether guys. You need to send a subscribe command before the `playMedia` command.

```
self.sendCommand('timeline/subscribe', **{'port': 32400, 'protocol': 'http', 'X-Plex-Device-Name': 'Python-PlexAPI'})

Then in the `playMedia` command also add the `X-Plex-Device-Name`:
https://github.com/pkkid/python-plexapi/blob/master/plexapi/client.py#L433-L441
```
        self.sendCommand('playback/playMedia', **dict({
            'machineIdentifier': self._server.machineIdentifier,
            'address': server_url[1].strip('/'),
            'port': server_url[-1],
            'offset': offset,
            'key': media.key,
            'token': media._server._token,
            'containerKey': '/playQueues/%s?window=100&own=1' % playqueue.playQueueID,
            'X-Plex-Device-Name': 'testing',
        }, **params))
```

I'm not sure what you'd like a comment on. Consider the following Markdown document: ``` 1. Line 1 1. Line 2 1. Line 3 ``` Converted to HTML: ``` .html
  1. Line 1
  2. Line 2
  3. Line 3
``` With CSS, you can change the number format: ``` .css ol {list-style-type: upper-roman;} ``` Now, arguably, if you wanted to convert only one list in the document (instead of all lists), you'd have to find a different way to style it. This might mean marking up the document a little differently to describe the intent of the section, such as: ``` ::: algorithm 1. Line 1 1. Line 2 1. Line 3 ::: ``` This becomes: ``` .html
  1. Line 1
  2. Line 2
  3. Line 3
``` And it's trivial to change the CSS for only that single list accordingly. ConTeXt is [syntactically different](http://wiki.contextgarden.net/Enumerations#Manual_labels), but the concept is the same.

The other part is that it's not a purely client-side choice. We do have an open PR (#112) to explicitly set the expiration, but there are a couple of nuances:

- This relies on the Redis cache backend calling `EXPIRE`, since we can't put backend-specific code here.
- Redis may not proactively evict "volatile" (e.g. has had `EXPIRE` called on it) keys right away, anyway. It's [more probablistic than that](https://redis.io/commands/expire). So for higher availability, you'll still probably want to make sure to set `maxmemory-policy` to `volatile-lru` or something.

Ultimately it's not the library's job to tell you how to set up your infrastructure—but what that really means is documenting how we ultimately expect it to behave and not making assumptions in the code.

The second commit, titled "Improve performance of carriage return replacement." was not meant to be in the same Pull Request, which is why it's not documented. My poor git skills betrayed me. Here is the explanation.

String manipulation with Python's `re` module is usually slower than achieving the same effect without using `re`. The [`re2`](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/re2/) library is a significantly faster in-place replacement for `re`, but would create a dependency for the core functionality of `python-markdown2`, which I don't think is desirable.

A brief look at this specific example (carriage return replacement) can be found [here](https://zindilis.com/blog/2015/11/07/regex-in-python-markdown2-pt1.html), and for completeness:

```python
In [1]: timeit 'Apples\r\nOranges\r\nKiwis\rGrapes\r'.replace('\r\n', '\n').replace('\r', '\n')
1000000 loops, best of 3: 404 ns per loop

In [2]: import re

In [3]: timeit re.sub(r'\r\n|\r', '\n', 'Apples\r\nOranges\r\nKiwis\rGrapes\r')
100000 loops, best of 3: 2.03 µs per loop
```

So `re` is about 5 times slower than `str.replace()`. Of course this is a negligible improvement in the total execution time of the conversion, but in time I'd like to look at more performance improvements for `python-markdown2`.

Hi,

I don't know if this is still a problem with recent versions of Django or Reversion, but I solved this issue for my company overriding the Django's JSON deserializer.

1. Create a module fixing the problem in the deserializer:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

from django.core.serializers.json import *
from django.core.serializers.python import _get_model
from django.utils.dateparse import parse_datetime
from django.db import models
from copy import deepcopy


def _fix_datefield_with_datetime_deserialization(objects):
    # Si a un DateField se le quiere deserializar un datetime,
    # se debe eliminar la parte del tiempo para evitar errores
    # del tipo: DeserializationError: [u"El valor '2018-06-04T00:00:00'
    # tiene formato de fecha inválido.  Debe ser de la forma YYYY-MM-DD."]
    new_objects = deepcopy(objects)
    models.get_apps()

    for i, d in enumerate(objects):
        model = _get_model(d['model'])

        for field_name, field_value in d['fields'].iteritems():
            # noinspection PyProtectedMember
            field = model._meta.get_field(field_name)

            if field_value and type(field) == models.DateField:
                parsed = parse_datetime(field_value)

                if parsed:
                    new_objects[i]['fields'][field_name] = parsed.date().isoformat()

    return new_objects


def Deserializer(stream_or_string, **options):
    """
    Deserialize a stream or string of JSON data.
    """
    if not isinstance(stream_or_string, (bytes, six.string_types)):
        stream_or_string = stream_or_string.read()
    if isinstance(stream_or_string, bytes):
        stream_or_string = stream_or_string.decode('utf-8')
    try:
        objects = json.loads(stream_or_string)
        objects = _fix_datefield_with_datetime_deserialization(objects)

        for obj in PythonDeserializer(objects, **options):
            yield obj

    except GeneratorExit:
        raise
    except Exception as e:
        # Map to deserializer error
        raise DeserializationError(e)

```
2. Use this serialization module on your settings.py

```python
SERIALIZATION_MODULES = {'json': 'common.json_serializers'}
```

It might be a bit hacky, but I found it to be the only feasible way for us to get rid of the problem. I hope it helps!

Nah, this takes the approach of transforming simpler fields - often hyperlink strings or PK ints - into complex, nested objects. Looks like JSON API instead appends the related objects in an `included` field. 

There is this [project](https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api), which offers some level of support for JSON API -- not sure about its support for dynamic embedding. 

My (small) team prefers the direct expansion approach; see [Stripe](https://stripe.com/docs/api#expanding_objects) for an example of a major API that does same. Though,  if you're working on a larger team, I can definitely see benefit of looking to a well-documented standard like JSON API, rather than trying to come up with and standardize on your own spec.

> lsusb shows it on bus 2 dev 8 but sysfs put it under 2-2:2 folder

This puzzled me a bit when I checked this too, but what sysfs represents is that the device is plugged in port n°2 of second USB bus - which is independent from the dynamically assigned USB address which happens to be 8 here (which depends on enumeration order and plug/unplug history).

> $ lsusb -v -s 2:8
[...]
> iManufacturer 1
[...]
> $ cat 2-2.2/manufacturer 
> Texas Instruments

Aha, so the string descriptor is actually present and could be retrieved by the kernel when enumerating the device. But for some reason, when libusb tried later to read string descriptor 1 (as retrieved from USB descriptor field iManufacturer), something timed out.

It may be a bug in device's firmware, or that python-libusb should not call libusb_get_string_descriptor_ascii for this.

By the way, could you paste the python-libusb error traceback ? python-libusb does not use kernel's cached string but actually tries to issue a USB request, for which it has to be able to open the device temporarily. Maybe the failure is not in trying to read the string descriptor, but actually when trying to open the device.

```python
    parent_lookup_kwargs = {
        # url lookup : ORM lookup
        'parent_pk': 'parent__pk',
        'parent2_pk': 'parent__parent_pk',
        'parent3_pk': 'parent__parent__parent_pk'
    }

This option would represent a compact configuration of the kwargs lookups vs. ORM lookups.

Working on a PR now. Hugo just uses different delimiters to specify the format. So

    ---
    format: yaml
    ---

    +++
    format: toml
    +++

    {
        "format": "json"
    }

That's pretty easy to implement, and that seems to be a convention that a few different static generators use, but it's not a real standard, so it might be troublesome if someone ever does it differently.

On the other hand, `frontmatter.json.load('json.md')` and `frontmatter.toml.load('toml.md')` don't really work very well either for the case where you don't know ahead of time which format you're dealing with (which is exactly the case I'm dealing with).

What I was planning was to add was a delimiter to format mapping that would default to the above but that you could override easily. Something like:

```python
delimiters = {'---': 'yaml', '+++': 'toml'}
post = frontmatter.load(myfile, delimiters=delimiters)```

That breaks down a little for something like the JSON delimiters that Hugo uses where it's '{' and '}', but basically heading somewhere in that direction.

import asyncio
import asyncio_redis as aioredis

@asyncio.coroutine
def go():

    print('connecting')
    conn = yield from aioredis.Pool.create(host='192.168.59.103', port=6379)

    cur.count = 1
    fet_count = 0

                                      # approximately same to the given count.
                                      # Please note that the count is just a hint, and
                                      # the actual number of fetched items may differ.
        if v is None: break
        print(v)
        fet_count += 1
    print('end, fetched {} items'.format(fet_count)) # the result is consistent
    conn.close()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(go())
    loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.sleep(0))
    loop.close()

I think weight decay is what you are looking for.

A small example is given below. You can just add `wd` in `mx.model.FeedForward.create`.

```r
require(mlbench)
require(mxnet)

data(BostonHousing, package = "mlbench")

train.ind <- seq(1, 506, 3)
train.x <- data.matrix(BostonHousing[train.ind,-14])
train.y <- BostonHousing[train.ind, 14]
test.x <- data.matrix(BostonHousing[-train.ind,-14])
test.y <- BostonHousing[-train.ind, 14]

data <- mx.symbol.Variable("data")
fc1 <- mx.symbol.FullyConnected(data, num_hidden = 1)
lro <- mx.symbol.LinearRegressionOutput(fc1)

mx.set.seed(0)
model <- mx.model.FeedForward.create(lro,
                                     X = train.x,
                                     y = train.y,
                                     ctx = mx.cpu(),
                                     num.round = 50,
                                     array.batch.size = 20,
                                     optimizer = "sgd",
                                     learning.rate = 2e-6,
                                     momentum = 0.9,
                                     wd = 0.1,
                                     eval.metric = mx.metric.rmse)
```

@apache/mxnet-committers: This issue has been inactive for the past 90 days. It has no label and needs triage.

For general "how-to" questions, our [user forum](https://discuss.mxnet.io/) (and [Chinese version](https://discuss.gluon.ai/)) is a good place to get help.

Upon further investigation it seems that the actual issue is that there is automatically generated default values for each argument:

https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/76bdc8772c7f4edd3087bd1285025122fc837c06/python/mxnet/ndarray/register.py#L67-L88

This results in the following function signature for the smooth_l1 operator:
```
def smooth_l1(data=None, scalar=_Null, out=None, name=None, **kwargs)
```
The problem is that these default values end up getting passed down to the C++ operator implementation and cause the error shown in the description. Ideally, we should only set default values that the user provided when they registered the operator.

If you follow the directions for contributions here:
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/tree/master/example#contributing
You will see the specifics for getting a notebook to appear on the docs website. You would need to submit a md file instead of a ipynb and put it in the tutorials folder. The md file needs specific text at the end for the download buttons.
One additional step would also to be to add a link to it to the index for the tutorials directory.
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/docs/tutorials/index.md - but make sure you use the version found here:

Thanks @bolkedebruin, yes celery 3 should still supported because the existing celery 3 style config setting names are kept intact. Also, loading via module name was supported celery 3.1 [method config_from_object called on config_source](http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/3.1/reference/celery.html#celery.Celery.config_from_object) 

Celery 4 is backwards compatible with celery 3 config names so upgrade to celery 4 will not be an issue either:
http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/latest/whatsnew-4.0.html#step-2-update-your-configuration-with-the-new-setting-names

However, if users want to use newer celery 4 style configs they probably should update all of the celery config, otherwise mixing old and new styles can throw an error: https://github.com/celery/celery/blob/master/celery/app/utils.py#L247

At `$JOB` we're wrapping sops in some higher-level tooling, which takes care of assuming the right role for a user working with secrets and populating the environment with appropriate AWS credentials before invoking sops.

We also provide a `--recover-secrets` flag for this tooling, which basically has the effect you're looking for here @jashandeep-sohi.  It does the simplest thing we could think of:  When `--recover-secrets` is given, we do not populate the environment with AWS credentials, which means sops's attempts to use KMS will fail and it will automatically fall back to PGP.

So even though this functionality is not built into sops, you can at least simulate it by manipulation of the environment in which sops is running.  Hope this helps!

I think you're using bleach in a way that's not going to work. Bleach operates on HTML--not on Markdown. I think you have a couple of options:

1. Convert the Markdown string to HTML and then have bleach operate on that.
2. Parse the Markdown and have bleach operate on parts of it that don't contain HTML-like things.

Of the two, I think you'll have most success with the first one.

```

The problem seems to be, that ```_namestack``` of the ```Unpickler``` gets reset while iterating through the keys/values of a dictionary in ```_restore_dict```.

Altering line 34 in ext/numpy.py from:
```python
return self.restore_dtype(data).type(self.context.restore(data['value']))
```
to:
```python
return self.restore_dtype(data).type(self.context.restore(data['value'], reset=False))
```
did the job for me. Probably need to do that for the NumpyNDArrayHandler as well.

Hope this saved anyone some time :)

As auditwheel is pre-installed in the manylinux1 docker container and can repair wheels both for Python 2 and Python 3, I am in favor of closing this issue. I like being able to use latest Python 3 features in this project and it's not a project that is meant to be use as library so there is no point in making it importable in a python 2 program.

As a side note, auditwheel should probably never be used outside the manylinux1 docker containers to repair Linux wheels (unless you really know what you do and read the PEP 513 spec).

Another weird thing is that the `_netCDF4` extension module has `RUNPATH` set to point to `$ORIGIN/.libs`, which is good, but then the vendored libraries like `.libs/libnetcdf4-...` have `RPATH` set instead (not `RUNPATH`).

(The difference between `RUNPATH` and `RPATH`: `RPATH` overrides `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`; `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` overrides `RUNPATH`. But if both are set, `RPATH` is ignored. `RPATH` is theoretically deprecated, if we care. The difference doesn't matter much to us, but I guess we should use `RUNPATH` consistently, because it reduces the chance of weird `RPATH`/`RUNPATH` interaction issues, it accords with the ld.so maintainers request to prefer `RUNPATH`, and I guess letting people use `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` to monkeypatch our search path is okay in extremis -- it shouldn't cause any problems in ordinary usage due to our use of unique mangled sonames.)

@meriki it looks like you were working on master. You should always work in a separate branch. (You can see instructions in the [contributing guide](http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/contribute.html#development-process) as well as Aaron Meurer's [Git workflow guide](http://www.asmeurer.com/git-workflow/).) Now that you've messed up your master branch (by messed up I mean you have commits that are different from our own master), you can clean it as follows:

- git remote add upstream https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image

This adds the official scikit-image repository to your git "address book".

- git fetch upstream

This downloads all of the branches from our official repository.

- git branch -m master messed-up-master

This renames your current branch "master" to a branch "messed-up-master"

- git checkout --track upstream/master

This checks out the official master branch into your own master branch.

- git checkout master -b fix-2551

Now you've made a branch specifically for this PR.

- git cherry-pick <commit>

Choose a commit that you want to apply that should go in the PR. `commit` should be the hash of a commit that you want to apply, like
d413bfa57b85e72daf7df2baabe5528f621a1c67
above.

Repeat for other commits that you want to add.

Then,

- git push --set-upstream origin fix-2551

And create a new pull request. In the meantime, I'll close this one because I don't think you can recover it. =P Good luck! =)

```python
    def thumbnail(self, size, resample=BICUBIC):
        """
        Make this image into a thumbnail.  This method modifies the
        image to contain a thumbnail version of itself, no larger than
        the given size.  This method calculates an appropriate thumbnail
        size to preserve the aspect of the image, calls the
        :py:meth:`~PIL.Image.Image.draft` method to configure the file reader
        (where applicable), and finally resizes the image.
        Note that this function modifies the :py:class:`~PIL.Image.Image`
        object in place.  If you need to use the full resolution image as well,
        apply this method to a :py:meth:`~PIL.Image.Image.copy` of the original
        image.
        :param size: Requested size.
        :param resample: Optional resampling filter.  This can be one
           of :py:attr:`PIL.Image.NEAREST`, :py:attr:`PIL.Image.BILINEAR`,
           :py:attr:`PIL.Image.BICUBIC`, or :py:attr:`PIL.Image.LANCZOS`.
           If omitted, it defaults to :py:attr:`PIL.Image.BICUBIC`.
           (was :py:attr:`PIL.Image.NEAREST` prior to version 2.5.0)
        :returns: None
        """

        # preserve aspect ratio
        x, y = self.size
        if x > size[0]:
            y = int(max(y * size[0] / x, 1))
            x = int(size[0])
        if y > size[1]:
            x = int(max(x * size[1] / y, 1))
            y = int(size[1])
        size = x, y

        if size == self.size:
            return

        self.draft(None, size)

        im = self.resize(size, resample)

        self.im = im.im
        self.mode = im.mode
        self.size = size

        self.readonly = 0
        self.pyaccess = None

Ok this is just my opinion. But to me the Object Model should be like this:

1. Structure. Columns and Cells. Should reside in maybe a Region object,
and should be shared between the SP and the TM.

2. Since each biological Layer has its own purpose and column/cells, they
should contain Regions which are the Column/Cell matrix.

3. The SP and TM should be treated as "Actions" - they are processes or
algorithms which modify the Columns and Cell structure (by adding distal
dendrites and synapses) and state (by altering permanences).

4. Structure should be separated from State. Structure includes Columns,
Cells, Proximal Dendrites and their Pooled connections. State is dynamic
and includes Distal Dendrites, Synapses on DistalDendrites (because they
grow and are culled), Permanences.

Cortex (Network)

   - Layer  (Contains References to SP TM's used
      - Region (Columns/Cells)
      - Proximal Dendrites
   - Layer
      - Region

...


SP and TM should hold no state, they should operate on the Columns and Cell
structure.

For reference, @aoman89757 is talking about this line: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/b03862e874a09cf4b39383d70965e2b63aa74a48/src/nupic/regions/CLAClassifierRegion.py#L380

I think you already know this, but the code's usage of the word "category" is very confusing. It's really just talking about buckets. It's not talking about the input to the a `CategoryEncoder`.

You're right, when you use `compute` instead of `customCompute`, it never passes the actual value into the classifier. So for scalar input, it's not taking advantage of the CLAClassifier's special handling for scalars. So the CLAClassifier will tell you a bucket, and you're responsible for converting that back into an actual value (e.g. through the encoder's `getBucketInfo` method). Note that through the network API the CLAClassifier is never receiving any actual input values -- that's why there's a `customCompute` method. It's not clear how we'd pass arbitrary actual values in through the Network API.

So the CLAClassifier is not full-featured when running through the network API. You can use it to classify to buckets (a.k.a. "categories"), but not to actual values. `getOutputData('actualValues')` is not useful. Only 'probabilities' and 'categoriesOut' are actually available.

The problem can be solved following this [patch](https://github.com/cernopendata/opendata.cern.ch/pull/1355/files), setting the `INVENIO_CELERY_BROKER_URL` env variable to the correct value. 

**Why?**
Essentially what was going wrong was that `CELERY_BROKER_URL`, which is the config variable for the broker url in Celery 4, was not being set and `invenio-celery` was [overwriting it with a default value](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-celery/blob/master/invenio_celery/config.py#L33) causing the Celery worker to try to connect to localhost.

A brief summary of past musings on this topic:

- for core second-order queries, the necessary information can be perhaps included first-hard in JSON-in-Elasticsearch, since expanded during JSON-in-PostgreSQL -> enhancer -> JSON-in-Elasticsearch step, kind of like [authorities](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-authorities/issues/1) are doing;

- for non-core second-order queries, and for any higher-order queries such as `refersto:refersto:refersto:higgs`, we'd most probably need to do a recursive expansion during runtime, just as in the Invenio legacy era. (Here, Solr 5 with its fast "intbitset" output would come handy.)

> this is *critical* for services such as INSPIRE [...] the *future* milestone

If this feels as a contradiction, then I think there are two things involved:

A. Collect ideas early to let them mature.
B. See who might liberate himself/herself to work on implementing them.

(Again, a bit like  [authorities](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-authorities/issues/1) that we started under phase A,  while the base stabilises enough to start also phase B.)

I guess @kaplun wanted to ticketise this topic early to get the ball rolling?

Sorry for not getting to this earlier! Yes, arrays of any size with a 0-length dimension is not supported, since it is not supported by HDF5. However, a scalar array could just be stored as an attribute, just like any Python and numpy scalar would (not scalar array, but just scalar, like `np.int16(100)`. This would cause a slight reconstruction discrepancy, since what you put in is not going to be exactly what you get back.

I rarely even come across scalar arrays in Python, so I'm not entirely certain how to support them properly and whether or not it would be OK to simply save them as scalars. It does seem like numpy scalar arrays and numpy scalars have slightly different semantics (http://stackoverflow.com/questions/773030/why-are-0d-arrays-in-numpy-not-considered-scalar), so does that mean it's important to unpack them correctly?

Maybe you could expand on why you want to save array scalars as opposed to regular scalars, since that could make a difference in how to support them. I'm leaning towards supporting them by extracting the scalar and saving it as an attribute (and unpacking it as a scalar). Possibly with a warning issued.

Hey @eldang, libpostal author here (Mapzen sponsored the project but otherwise it's independent). You might be interested in giving the 1.0 release a try. Just came out Friday. The original version had relatively poor/mislabeled address data for Colombia related to some inconsistencies in OSM, but in the latest release we were able to get two mostly-complete data sets from Bogotá and Medellín into the [OpenAddresses](https://github.com/openaddresses/openaddresses) project (which is also now imported for libpostal). I've added some Colombia-specific patterns to the training data as well. The new release uses a more powerful ML model (Conditional Random Fields/Viterbi inference instead of the greedy model previously used) and generally has training data for every inhabited country on Earth ([here's the breakdown by country and data set](https://airtable.com/tblrnJ0WTs38zlvPv/viw3Wd6G2iRE0OtDc)) totalling over 1 billion examples globally.

Colombia example with the latest parser:

```
> CR 18#63-64 B Chapinero Bogotá DC Colombia

Result:

{
  "road": "cr 18",
  "house_number": "#63-64 b",
  "city_district": "chapinero",
  "city": "bogotá dc",
  "country": "colombia"
}

> CR 18 63 64 B Chapinero Bogotá DC Colombia

Result:

{
  "road": "cr 18",
  "house_number": "63 64 b",
  "city_district": "chapinero",
  "city": "bogotá dc",
  "country": "colombia"
}
```

Also handles the other countries mentioned. Here's China:

```
> 中国，山东省，青岛市 香港东路6号，5号楼，8号室 李小方 先生收

Result:

{
  "country": "中国",
  "state": "山东省",
  "city": "青岛市",
  "road": "香港东路",
  "house_number": "6号",
  "level": "5号楼",
  "unit": "8号室",
  "house": "李小方 先生收"
}
```

and some more complex formats in Russia:

```
> 197198, г. Санкт-Петербург, ул. Съезжинская д. 10 кв. 40

Result:

{
  "postcode": "197198",
  "city": "г. санкт-петербург",
  "road": "ул. съезжинская",
  "house_number": "д. 10",
  "unit": "кв. 40"
}
```

Feel free to raise issues on https://github.com/openvenues/libpostal directly if there are problems. It's not a static project and improves over time. Most issues/patterns can be fixed in the training data at this point (incidentally the [~100GB of training data is also now public](https://github.com/openvenues/libpostal/blob/master/README.md#training-data) so it should be easy to grep for specific addresses or patterns).

I wrote code that properly calculates the sortino ratio and checked it against the example in the Red Rock paper (output below). 

One thing I'm not sure about is annualizing, they used annual returns in their example but we use daily returns.

Any thoughts @justinlent 
```
def downside_risk(returns, required_return=0):
    downside_diff = returns - required_return
    mask = downside_diff > 0
    downside_diff[mask] = 0.0
    squares = np.square(downside_diff)
    mean_squares = np.nanmean(squares, axis=0)
    return np.sqrt(mean_squares)

def sortino_ratio(returns, required_return=0):
    mu = np.nanmean(returns - required_return, axis=0)
    return mu / downside_risk(returns, required_return)

>>> returns = pd.Series([17, 15, 23, -5, 12, 9, 13, -4]) / 100.
>>> print sortino_ratio(returns)
4.4172610429938617
```

I noticed another little issue at [here](https://github.com/quantopian/alphalens/blob/new-api/alphalens/utils.py#L306). When calling `util.quantize_factor` it is possible that some of the factor values are dropped, depending on the quantiles/bins arguments. This is a feature, as it could be useful to drop top/bottom values as a measure to remove outliers. So we don't want to keep factor entries whose values are NaN, this means a second call to 
`merged_data = merged_data.dropna()` is required after calling `util.quantize_factor`

And the previous comment makes me think we might want to test few specific numpy versions too, as we do we pandas. So that we are sure Alphalens works fine with a specific subset of numpy/pandas/python versions, currently we are only sure about pandas/python versions.

My idea would be to add a flag to create_factor_tear_sheet so that the user can supply the binning type (quantile or equal interval). We don't need any other api changes but we need a function similar to performance.quantize_factor that accepts the same input but returns the factor binned by value intervals instead of quantiles. We then call performance.quantize_factor or the new function depending on the binning type flag.

-U can implicitly upgrade child dependencies if they're not locked in. To be clear, there's nothing broken with Sentry here, its something specific to your install so you're going to have to debug. If you do what I said (re-install the deps) things should be correct. You can also 'pip freeze' to see the versions of things, and while that error message doesnt provide enough context my first guess would be an invalid version of Django.

_We_ don't ignore it specifically, but it's not caught by `Exception` since it's stemming from `BaseException`, and Django specifically ignores it, so it's never caught by its signal handler.

We specifically miss it here: https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/middleware.py#L36, https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/middleware.py#L43, and https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/middleware.py#L52 since it's not an `Exception`.

My proposal would be to basically add:

```python
except Exception:
    self.handle_exception(environ)
except SystemExit:
    self.handle_exception(environ)
    raise
```

> Finally, the normalization of ChromaticObjects that aren't attached to an SED is done via lambdas, which means that fewer things are pickable than previously. I'm not sure if this merits more attention or not.

[This page](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/25348532/can-python-pickle-lambda-functions) claims `dill` enables pickling of lambda functions.  It's pip-installable, so a low bar for a dependency.  Might be worth looking into.  As we move towards having Chromatic objects run in config, I think it might become useful to have them be picklable.

in lsst_wcs.py, then `scons tests` runs through to completion (albeit with some expected failures).  Without these lines commented, it reliably freezes when using 4 processes.

regarding timeouts/retries:  I think it makes a lot more sense for retries to be configured by the script author with each method.  How many retries are reasonable  ... and whether retries are reasonable at all ... depends on what exactly is happening in the script.  For example, imagine that replicas are being provisioned via ZFS snapshot export.  If that failed, something really wonky could be wrong, and you wouldn't want to retry it.  For some scripts, the user is going to want to retry only on specific error codes.

Connexion uses Werkzeug for routing (indirectly via Flask). According to [Rule.match_compare_key](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/werkzeug/blob/5cdc596fd1b725cd25b457072e1f545958c78226/werkzeug/routing.py#L777):

> rules without any arguments come first for performance reasons only as we expect them to match faster and some common ones usually don't have any arguments (index pages etc.)

So I'm almost sure that `/user/insert` will be prioritized, but I never tested it.

@kyleknap I should have clarified in this PR, the main thing I was focused on here was the interfaces across all the objects (planner, remote state, etc) and not necessarily the implementation.  I do think that once more resources are added (API Gateway, cloudwatch events, etc) that new interfaces will be created that allows functionality to be sliced per-resource.  That would mean that the existing interfaces would still be the same, but the implementation would probably delegate out to something that's resources specific.  The thing I'm worried about right now is that if I try to extract out these components right now, there might be things I'm missing as new resources get added.  What I'd prefer to do is:

1. Get update support added
2. Get delete support added
3. Add support for one or two more resources (probably API Gateway and either cloudwatch events or built-in authorizers)
4. Extract out functionality to be per-resource.

As an example, I suspect that the final version of these resources might look something like this

```python
class LambdaFunctionResource:
    model = resource.awslambda.LambdaFunction
    planner = resource.awslambda.LambdaPlanner
    remote_state = resource.awslambda.RemoteState
```

and perhaps the planner implementation code would now look like:

```python
class Planner(object):
    def execute(self, resources):
        ...
        for resource in resources:
            plan.append(resource.planner.plan())
```

Again this probably isn't exactly what it'll look like but I do plan on extracting things out eventually.

I'm not certain what the differences between "hang" and "drop" are, but I'll describe the symptoms in more detail.

In both Java and Go (and probably others) without keepalive, the stream just never give us new message or notify us of any errors.
In Java, where we give gRPC callbacks (`onNext`, `onError`, and `onCompleted`) to handle events, none of the callbacks are ever called.
In Go, where we call `msg, err = stream.Recv()` the method never returns, giving us neither messages nor errors.

With keepalive configured, we'll eventually get an error (from experience, either `UNAVAILABLE` or `INTERNAL`) and then we can re-create the stream.

```
t.add_output(
    Output(
        "StageOut",
        Description="desc",
        Value=If("InProd", "Prod", "Not Prod")
    )
)
```

@benjyw This is so developers can use known paths in unpacked distributions for building native extensions. For example, we have an internal team that wants to build tensorflow extensions and can get .so files and headers to link against in two ways: tf.sys_config.get_include(), or using a path join off of `os.path.join(os.environ['PYTHONPATH'], 'tensorflow', 'include'). So essentially you can use known paths off the site dir to reference headers/object files in your setup script. Ideally, one would use the python API to get these paths, however the TF case requires using `os.environ` because one of its deps (google.protobuf) uses unique namespaced .pth file magic that breaks the import machinery when placed on the PYTHONPATH env variable.

```yaml
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31

Description: Lambda that responds to S3 events

Parameters:
    PreExistingBucket:
        Description: "Does an existing bucket exist (not managed by cloudformation)"
        Type: String
        Default: 'no'
        AllowedValues:
            - 'yes'
            - 'no'

This will generally be easier and more reliable than scanning the styles.xml unless you know a little more about what you're looking for.

One note about the current implementation: The decision whether to use twisted is a global one, and it is decided when the module is imported for the first time. Afterwards, this cannot be changed.
This is unfortunate, since you might have one script using the normal version and one using the twisted version - and you get errors if you try to run them consecutively on the same interpreter.

The only solution I see for this problem is to reverse the dependencies: make the classes in events.py abstract base classes, and the classes in events_base(_twisted).py inherit from them. Then the user could import events_base or events_base_twisted as he likes. (Of course, the module names would be different then.)

'announce route-refresh' clearly does not do what you believe it does. It is its own RFC. It does not announce any route, the syntax should be something like 'announce route-refresh ipv4 unicast', which will force a re-announcement of the routes in the ADJ-RIB-OUT to the peer again.

The index (which make a route unique) for a ipvpn route is:
```
	def index (self, negotiated=None):
		addpath = 'no-pi' if self.path_info is PathInfo.NOPATH else self.path_info.pack()
		mask = chr(len(self.rd)*8 + self.cidr.mask)
		return NLRI._index(self) + addpath + mask + self.rd.pack() + self.cidr.pack_ip()
```

So two routes with different labels are considered the same. I believe it is what the RFC specifies, so the second one is ignored. 

The best way to prevent this issue for you would be to disable the ADJ-RIB-OUT, using `adj-rib-out false` on the peer.

> How is this used?

Client needs to allocate arrays large enough for the number of channels specified in the channelName input. So "" means "all the channels in the session, based on when you opened the session". "0-3" means "4 channels". "0, 1" means 2 channels.

* **Option 1**: Don't support niDCPower_MeasureMultiple() in Python. We need to evaluate how bad this would be and what the workarounds are.
    * **Option 1b**: ... until a better alternative exists in NI-DCPower.
* **Option 2**: Implement channel parsing logic in Python so it matches the NI-DCPower driver. This would be error prone, and a shame.
* **Option 3**: Use an internal, secret, private function in NI-DCPower that does the parsing for you. This exists and is leverage by the C# API which has the same problem. But that's a closed source API. Do we want to use a private function in our Open Source Python wrappers? Ideally no.

I think we should prioritize "easy to build models" over "easy to use pytorch RNNs".  The way it currently is, my `model.forward()` has to have two different representations of the mask, and an imported function to compute one from the other, even though I'm just using `Seq2SeqEncoders`, which is a construct we invented.  I think we should pick one of these representations and use it everywhere, and the binary mask seems like a much more natural representation to use.

If I'm using a `Seq2SeqEncoder`, I've already bought into the AllenNLP API.  If I want to switch to a plain pytorch RNN, I have to change the way I'm constructing the encoder anyway - it's not that hard to require also importing a function and converting a mask.  But using the API should be the easy case, not the hard case.

> The docstring for the class VoiceLeadingQuartet indicates that v1 should be the top voice

That's something I added today, since now there are now methods that assume that.  I'm not sure it was required or even encouraged before.  I believe I was usually doing the opposite, but it's easily changed.

> isProperResolution() and having any coherent notion of voice crossing depend on a required ordering of the voices. We might also think about the case of analysis of two equal voices that cross a lot, in which case the notion of a higher voice fades away.

I think that since we didn't enforce or give any warnings for people in the past, we might add routines for `higherVoice()`, `lowerVoice()` which simply gets whichever has a higher n1, with n2 as a tie-breaker, and use those in cases where we assume one voice is higher than the other.  In which case voiceCrossing would only be true if `hi_voice_n2 < lo_voice_n2`. But I haven't given much thought to it.

  > isProperResolution(), opensIncorrectly(), closesIncorrectly() etc. These are all style specific, and should probably be renamed(?).

I really don't like these terms because they imply we "should" be composing in/analyzing specific styles and the module is more general than that.  Usually we put in a deprecation notice for a while (1 year; remove in version 6 or at latest 7) and have the function call the correct one.  Or we leave the names alone and put in a style/genre flag:  "isProperResolution(style=styleEnum.MODAL_COUNTERPOINT)" and then at least we're making explicit what we mean and we can possibly add other styles later -- probably needing another method anyhow.

Rendering `DECIMAL(38, 0)` as a `string` is more data-sciency because it does not loose precision for a type that is often used for categorization, and enables you to do whatever you please. Also, since most data science uses 8-byte types (or smaller ones), I opted for a good solution for anything that fits into 8 bytes of precision (such as `DECIMAL(18, 0)`).

Your automatic casting seems like a sensible approach, but I am not opposed to give turbodbc users the option to choose what to do for larger decimals. Alternatively, you could just cast the column to `DOUBLE` or `INTEGER` depending on the precision you really need for this field.

`Decimal` would maintain precision, that is true. `unixodbc` offers 128 bits of precision with its `NUMERIC` and `DECIMAL` data types. That is sufficient for MSSQL, where `NUMERIC(38, 0)` seems to be the maximum. It is not sufficient for MySQL that supports `NUMERIC(65, 0)` or PostgreSQL (`DECIMAL(1000, 0)`). In these cases, values need to be retrieved as strings in any case. And I also want to avoid other stuff such as `DECIMAL(18, 0)` to be retrieved as a `DECIMAL`, because it perfectly fits in a 64 bit integer without lying at all.
This should be extended to also handle cases where there is not enough disk space.

Ok, but each `PeerPoolSubscriber` doesn't want to maintain its own version of a blacklist to answer that `is_valid_connection_candidate()` call, does it?

Can you give a rundown how you anticipate some common use cases.

Here are some from my book:

**Tx Pool wants to block a peer**

1. `TxPool` recognizes `bad_peer` has fed us junk too often (using `TxPool` internal accounting)
2. `TxPool` calls `self.blocked_peer_service.block_peer(bad_peer)`
3. `BlockedPeerService` propagates `PeerBlocked` event to everyone whos listening for it
4. `PeerPool` who is among the listeners, recognizes the new blocked peer and disconnects.

**New Peer Joins the Pool**
1. A new peer joins the pool but the PeerPool checks `self.blocked_peer_service.is_blocked(new_peer)` before it lets it in and propagates the new peer to `PeerPoolSubscriber`

Installing packages on a system where `#!/bin/bash` isn't available but `#!/usr/bin/env bash` wouldn't work because of:
https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/install.py#L339

The same applies for many of the packages offered by Anaconda as well as many of the recipes at
https://github.com/conda/conda-recipes/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=bash

There is a way to set environment variables when activating a conda environment.  To do this, there need to be a bash script in `<PREFIX>/etc/conda/activate.d/*.sh`.  When `source activate` is run, all these scripts are "sourced".  For example, a conda package could have `etc/conda/activate.d/foo.sh`.

This is a conda issue:  
https://github.com/Anaconda-Platform/anaconda-client/issues/380

Yes, everything is BSD (except for progressbar, which is LGPL). 

I wasn't referring to legal requirements. The various distros have many technical requirements for packages in their repos. For instance, would they be OK with conda installing its own Python, rather than using the Debian, etc. Python? Debina has a [huge](https://www.debian.org/doc/debian-policy/) document of requirements. 

You're welcome to try. I don't think it's something that Continuum should really be spending its resources trying to do. We benefit from building our own ecosystem (which is open source and extendable by others), so that we are not restricted by arbitrary rules from other authorities. The recommended way to install conda on Linux is and will remain installing Anaconda or Miniconda.

For anyone coming to this thread now, it unfortunately turns out that backing out of this isn't all that easy. You actually need to back up to your previous version of conda, and then you can update to conda latest (right now `4.0.4`).

The output of

    ls $(/conda/bin/conda info | grep cache | cut -d: -f2) | grep -E "^conda-\d"

should show you the version numbers available in your cache. Then from there, for example if `3.19.3` was the version number indicated

    conda install conda=3.19.3

If you are not providing an id there is no benefit in using the `create` API and you should instead be using the `index` API which still provides the functionality.

The only benefit of the `create` API is if you have specified an ID the operation will fail if a document with that ID already exists. This is not applicable without an ID value so the parameter has been switched to required.

Here is a function of the create_deployment and create_vm_deployment 
def create_deployment(config):
    sub_id = config.get_subscription_id()
    cert_path = config.get_subscription_certificate()

    service_cert = config.get_service_certificate_path()
    service_cert_thumb = config.get_service_certificate_thumbprint()

    storage_account = config.get_cloud_service_storage_account()
    storage_container = config.get_cloud_service_storage_container()

    ret = []

    for cs in config.get_cloud_services():
        cs_name = config.get_prefix() + '-' + cs['name']
        cs_loc = cs['location']

        # check if the cloud service already exists
        if lib.azure_vm.is_cloud_service_exists(sub_id, cert_path, cs_name):
            # if so, delete it first
            logger.info('Cloud service %s already exists, deleting ...' % cs_name)

            lib.azure_vm.delete_cloud_service(sub_id, cert_path, cs_name)


        # create the cloud service
        lib.azure_vm.create_cloud_service(sub_id,
                                        cert_path,
                                        cs_name,
                                        cs_loc,
                                        service_cert)

        for vm in cs['virtual_machines']:
            vm_name = config.get_prefix() + '-' + vm['hostname']
            vm_size = vm['size']
            vm_username = vm['username']
            vm_password = vm['password']

            # look up image name
            vm_image = config.get_image_name(vm['os_image'])

            lib.azure_vm.create_vm_deployment(sub_id,
                                cert_path,
                                cs_name,
                                service_cert_thumb,
                                storage_account,
                                storage_container,
                                vm_image,
                                vm_username,
                                vm_password,
                                vm_name,
                                vm_size)

        eps = lib.azure_vm.wait_vm_endpoint(sub_id, cert_path, cs_name, cs_name)
        ret += eps

    # add data disks if any
    for cs in config.get_cloud_services():
        cs_name = config.get_prefix() + '-' + cs['name']
        for vm in cs['virtual_machines']:
            vm_name = config.get_prefix() + '-' + vm['hostname']
 
            vm_data_disks = vm['data_disks']
            lun = 0
            for d in vm_data_disks:

                timestr = str(datetime.datetime.now().hour) + '_' + str(datetime.datetime.now().minute) + '_' + str(datetime.datetime.now().second)
                dd_name = vm_name + '_' + timestr + '_DD_' + str(d) + '_' + str(lun) + '.vhd'
                r = lib.azure_vm.add_data_disk(sub_id,
                                        cert_path,
                                        cs_name,
                                        vm_name,
                                        storage_account,
                                        storage_container,
                                        dd_name,
                                        d,
                                        lun)
                if r == 'Succeeded':
                    lun = lun + 1
                else:
                    raise Exception('Failed to add data disk: %s' % r)

    # TODO: wait on VMs again to make sure they are in "ReadyRole" and add future
    # verification that SSH/Pyro4 ports are open. Adding disks could cause VMs to reboot.

    return ret

def create_vm_deployment(subscription_id,      # subscription ID
        certificate_path,           # path to Azure management certificate (pem)
        cloud_service_name, 
        ssh_certificate_thumbprint, # SHA1 thumbprint of SSH certificate in the cloud service
        storage_account,
        storage_container,
        os_image_name,              # OS image name, retrieved by /services/images
        user_name,
        password,
        host_name,
        vm_size):
    
    sms = azure.servicemanagement.ServiceManagementService(subscription_id, certificate_path)

    vm_name = host_name 

    logger.info('Creating VM %s of size %s ...' % (vm_name, vm_size))

    # media_link for OS VHD
    timestr = str(datetime.datetime.now().hour) + '_' + str(datetime.datetime.now().minute) + '_' + str(datetime.datetime.now().second)
    media_link = 'https://' + storage_account + '.blob.core.windows.net/' + storage_container + '/' + vm_name + '_' + timestr + '_OS.vhd'

    # Linux configuration set
    linux_config = azure.servicemanagement.LinuxConfigurationSet(host_name, user_name, password, True)

    # SSH public key
    pk = azure.servicemanagement.PublicKey(ssh_certificate_thumbprint, '/home/' + user_name + '/.ssh/authorized_keys')
    pair = azure.servicemanagement.KeyPair(ssh_certificate_thumbprint, '/home/' + user_name + '/.ssh/id_rsa')
    linux_config.ssh.public_keys.public_keys.append(pk)
    linux_config.ssh.key_pairs.key_pairs.append(pair)

    os_hd = azure.servicemanagement.OSVirtualHardDisk(os_image_name, media_link)

    # endpoint configuration set, add SSH port
    ep = azure.servicemanagement.ConfigurationSet()

    ep.input_endpoints.input_endpoints.append(azure.servicemanagement.ConfigurationSetInputEndpoint('SSH', 'TCP', str(random.randint(50000, 60000)), '22'))
    ep.input_endpoints.input_endpoints.append(azure.servicemanagement.ConfigurationSetInputEndpoint('Pyro4', 'TCP', str(random.randint(50000, 60000)), '50000', idle_timeout_in_minutes=30))

    # get deployment
    # if the deployment already exists, use AddRole to create new VM, otherwise we should use CreateVirtualMachineDeployment
    new_vm = False
    try:
        deployment = sms.get_deployment_by_name(cloud_service_name, cloud_service_name)
    except azure.WindowsAzureMissingResourceError:
        new_vm = True

    if new_vm == True:
        result = sms.create_virtual_machine_deployment(service_name = cloud_service_name,
                                                    deployment_name = cloud_service_name, 
                                                    deployment_slot = 'production',
                                                    label = cloud_service_name,
                                                    role_size = vm_size,
                                                    role_name = vm_name,
                                                    system_config = linux_config,
                                                    network_config = ep,
                                                    os_virtual_hard_disk = os_hd)
    else:
        result = sms.add_role(service_name = cloud_service_name,
                            deployment_name = cloud_service_name,
                            role_name = vm_name,
                            system_config = linux_config,
                            os_virtual_hard_disk = os_hd,
                            network_config = ep,
                            role_size = vm_size)


    # wait for the async operation to succeed
    request_id = result.request_id

    for i in range(60):
        status = get_operation_status(subscription_id, certificate_path, request_id)

        if status.status == 'Succeeded':
            break
        if status.status == 'Failed':
            print status.error.message
            break

        time.sleep(1)

    result = sms.get_deployment_by_name(cloud_service_name, cloud_service_name)
    logger.info('Deployment ID: %s' % result.private_id)
    return result.private_id
