The first section on [pre-commit.com](https://pre-commit.com) briefly covers this but I'll go in more depth :)

- pre-commit aims to need _zero_ things installed at the system level -- that is, I shouldn't need a sysadmin to manage ruby / scss-lint / flake8 / etc. at the system level in order to use industry-standard linters in a project
- projects encode the desired versions in a pre-commit configuration and they are (mostly) repeatable.  this means that the repositories linters will continue to pass irrelevant of external changes (whether they be system changes, or external changes).  This necessarily means that each repository may choose a different version of linters.
- in the python ecosystem, `virtualenv` is the natural choice for creating isolated environments to install python packages in.

pre-commit also aims to be a *user* tool, and not a system tool -- the linters it installs are all installed in user space and shouldn't interfere with the system.

I did a bit of looking into how Mozilla handles this, and I'll leave some links here for reference:

They collect histograms: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Mozilla/Performance/Adding_a_new_Telemetry_probe

And send them back as JSON documents (~one per day): https://ci.mozilla.org/job/mozilla-central-docs/Tree_Documentation/

Public aggregated data: https://telemetry.mozilla.org

The originally included [no unique installation id](https://blog.mozilla.org/tglek/2011/05/13/firefox-telemetry/), but eventually [changed their mind](http://robertovitillo.com/2014/12/19/clientid-in-telemetry-submissions/) and started including one (except for specific data that is deemed too sensitive and thus split off into separate unlabeled submissions), because they found the [restriction was too cumbersome](https://mail.mozilla.org/pipermail/firefox-dev/2014-December/002580.html).

One thing I was surprised by, though I guess it makes sense, is that they *unconditionally* send a ping (complete with unique per-installation ID) at (1) first startup, (2) if/when a user opts *out* of telemetry. (And these pings include some basic data -- Firefox version, OS, etc.) I guess this makes sense -- there isn't really much chance of a privacy violation in knowing that "someone somewhere installed Firefox and that's all they want us to know", and it's crucial to extrapolating from the data they do get to the overall user population.

An internal planning document with lots of interesting details on e.g. metrics they want to track: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1IGpzsYGi_sq3YFQDAPyKOkU_BKvXAC95fZYA2i4ceVs/edit#

On Mon, Nov 23, 2015 at 10:55 AM, Brian E. Granger <notifications@github.com
> wrote:

> The most important cell is the one that has focus. Traditionally we have
> called this cell the "selected" cell. It has two purposes or roles:
>
> A) It maintains focus and is the cell that will be edited if the user
> presses "Enter".
> B) By default cell level actions apply to it.
>
> Previously, we used a single visual indicator for both of these roles (grey
> border).
>
> In the new multiple-selection behavior we have agreed upon, these two roles
> are being separated.
>
> * The focused cell has both of these roles.
> * Contiguous blocks of cells (that include the focused cell) can also have
> the second role.
>
> Important things should be indicated as such visually. Less important
> things should be indicated as such visually.
>
> In my mind, even with the new behaviors, the focused cell is the most
> important one. Actions always apply to to, it is the cell that will be
> edited, the page will scroll as the user moves it and it is the cursor/head
> for multiple selections. Because of this, it should have the most prominent
> visual design.
>
> The other cells to which actions will apply should have a less prominent
> visual design than the focused cell as they do fewer things.
>

I think we agree here... My only concern is that I still think we need to
make sure the users don't easily miss a long range of selected cells, given
the potential for large-scale impact with large selections.

My problem with the current design is that the visual design of the action
> cells is the equivalent of yelling. The extremely strong visual treatment
> of these cells is inconsistent with the more subtle design choices we make
> everywhere else *and* it is confusingly more prominent than that focused
> cell.
>

Question: can we still get to the goal of ensuring it's impossible to miss
large selections with a whole-cell highlight but one that's much more
subdued?

Again, let's keep in mind that these highlights are *fleeting* by nature,
they don't stay in the UI permanently, so I think it's OK for them to be a
bit stronger (and that's why editors make them quite strong, in fact). But
I am definitely listening to the notion that you want it more subdued.

I also ask that we *experiment* with using it, rather than continuing to
argue with about it... To get things to a concrete form where we can play
with it, would the following be agreeable?

- A more subdued color for the highlight, and let's mark clearly where in
the CSS that value is, so we can all easily play with it on our own.  That
way, we can experiment and see if consensus is not even in the cards...

- A clear cursor distinction for the selected cell has to remain.

How does that sound? We play with it, we experiment with a more subdued
(but broad) highlight and with how we feel the clarity of the cursor
highlight maintains that distinction, and see if we can find a working
solution?

I agree there's differences in the behavior we offer, and this has been a
useful discussion to clarify that. I don't think we're unique from other
UIs, but we are a different combination of the behaviors that exist
elsewhere, and it's great to clarify that.  Honestly, once we're done here
we should write up a little short summary of these basic design constraints
to remind ourselves of the key points, since it's useful to have them all
in one place.

@lkraider so in tackling that repr issue, I found another fun issue. I've resolved it to a pretty good point, but I wanted to make note of it.

the str_compat decorator on classes was interfering with the ability to make nested repr calls. In the case of CompoundError in Python2, the ErrorMessage class would be converted to a unicode-escaped string, which would then be double-escaped when the BaseError escapes ITS __repr__ call.

Removing str_compat form BaseError and ErrorMessage resolves the issue, but I imagine it loses the __str__ and __unicode__ behavior that was expected in the first place. Thus, I split the str_compat into str_compat and repr_compat. I ran into a weird recursive import with attempting to use repr_compat on the model. I put in a quick workaround, but I'll take a closer look if I can. 

Ultimately, the rational for operating on __str__ / __unicode__ makes sense to me, but attempting to escape __repr__ makes nested repr calls impossible, and it's unnecessary as long as __repr__ overrides compose their parts out of other __repr__ calls (such as how BaseError / ErrorMessage now works).

At this point this addresses all the feedback, and maintains as much compatibility as possible (the broken places were already mentioned above, like mutability).

it might be fun to tackle the repr_compat issue across all of schematics in a separate PR. But if desired I can do it in this one.

Another thing I'm thinking about is execution order. We don't document this, but rule fields execute in the order given [here](https://github.com/buildinspace/peru#rules):

1. copy
2. move
3. pick
4. executable
5. export

A new `drop` field (or whatever) would probably go either right before or right after `pick`. Do we care which it is? I'm having trouble thinking of a case where the order matters. (Remember that both fields support globs.)

It's also possible to put it after `export`, and we considered that with `pick` to, but I think we want to stick with before for consistency with `copy`/`move`. That way all the path arguments in `peru.yaml` are full paths.

Working thru tutorial... note to self -- left off at "Adding the scheduler"

Random numbers will never be deterministic in a multi-threaded environment (since the order of requests for random numbers is then based on thread scheduling).  I don't think double-seeding the global random seeds would be problematic, either - it's redundant and not the Mesa best practice, but it would work.

This PR doesn't *preclude* a Mesa user using their own state instances (and seeding those instances if they want repeatable results).

I'm going by the [Zen of Python's](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0020/) "simple is better than complex" -- in this case, the complex option involves threading two RandomState instances (one for python, one for numpy) through models, agents, servers, etc., without hurting compatibility.

> Just to make sure I understood, @davehunt, In "We also lose the handling of images that prevents large data URLs to be duplicated in the HTML", what do you exactly means by duplicated?

Currently the images are represented as base64 encoded data URLs, which are very large strings. We use this string for the `img` tag's `src` attribute, but also for the two link destinations. We're using JavaScript to pull the `src` of the image and use it as the `href` for the links, therefore avoiding repeating the long data URL.

I think this issue is actually little tricky. Just to make sure everyone is on the same page including myself, this question is asked when requesting a new certificate for a subset of domains found in a lineage. This can be a strict subset or identical names to an existing lineage.

This is actually quite subtle. Imaging you have a lineage for example.com, example.org, and example.net and then run the client requesting a cert for example.com and example.org (excluding example.net). What should the client do? Should it update the existing lineage? If so, it may break a server for example.net. If it doesn't update the existing lineage, the configurations for example.com and example.net need to be updated.

With that said, I think there is value in adding some of the functionality @pde proposed above. The one choice I'm not sure I agree with is **\<Reinstall\>**. The problem is this question comes up during authentication, not installation. I believe we have correctly been trying to make `run` the equivalent of `certonly` followed by `install` (see #771). This moves in the opposite direction as allowing the user to choose **\<Reinstall\>** when running `certonly` doesn't make sense.

The one thing we might be able to do is provide something like a **\<Keep existing cert\>** option (hopefully with better wording). Probably a little early to jump into implementation details, but the way I see this working is if **\<Keep existing cert\>** is chosen, `_auth_from_domains()` in `cli.py` simply returns the existing lineage without any modification. If this is done, `certonly` will return with no further effect and `run` will give that certificate to the `installer`.

On the subject of signing—or at least repeatability:

* PyPI does not allow package authors to change old packages, and it's not customary to delete old version.
* [Peep](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/peep/) is a tool a wrote to verify the hashes of PyPI packages before installing them. We use it to do deployments at Mozilla. It saves having to manage a vendor lib or run a private index server and protects against PyPI compromises and MITMs.
* Peep does not solve VCS-sourced dependencies completely, though GitHub ones have a nice workaround: [linking to the GitHub-generated zip of a revision](https://github.com/mozilla/dxr/blob/master/requirements.txt#L50). BitBucket [has the same workaround](https://github.com/mozilla/dxr/blob/master/requirements.txt#L47).
* If we use any git checkouts as part of our release process, we should be sure to run `git fsck` against them. At least as of a few versions ago, git didn't check hashes automatically on clone, so a compromised remote repo could just hand you whatever it wanted.

Not sure if your app was working properly or why you had the need to hash type. Apparently if you override `__eq__`, you also must override `__hash__` (see "[Types that define `__eq__` are unhashable?](https://stackoverflow.com/a/1608882/4759433)"). Also, the `__hash__` default implementation seems to rely on the ID of the object, so your code should never show that two items were the same as new objects are always created (thus in likely different memory locations), as mentioned in "[What is the default \_\_hash\_\_ in python?](https://stackoverflow.com/q/11324271/4759433)".

Since `__hash__` is supposed to be fast (I guess?) I'm not going to implement this on the library because it's rather costly. You can however do the following:
```python
from telethon.tl import TLObject


def my_hash(self):
    d = self.to_dict()
    return hash(tuple(tuple(v) if isinstance(v, list) else v
                      for k, v in sorted(d.items(), key=lambda kv: kv[0])))


TLObject.__hash__ = my_hash
```

Then all TLObjects (including `Message`) should be hashable, but it might be slow if the object has a lot of other nested objects or long lists (which are not hashable and need to be a tuple).

The two features are independent.  Both are ways to delete files that are older than you want.

When you run `b2 sync --keepDays 7 ...`, it will delete files that have been hidden for more than 7 days.  If you have a lifecycle rules with `daysFromHidingToDeleting` set to 7, it has the same effect.

The difference is when the rules are applied.  The option to sync applies just to when you sync.  If you stop syncing, it will stop cleaning up old files.  Lifecycle rules, on the other hand, are applied automatically once a day by the Backblaze servers.

For this feature we need support from the API. Currently it's not possible
to get the part hashes of large files after the upload has finished.

What doesn't work about it?

The `*` in a trigger usually *has* to match something or else the trigger won't match the user's message. If you had `who is *`, the user has to say "who is (something)", if they just said "who is" it won't match that trigger. The exception is when you put a `*` in square brackets, like `[*]` which makes it an optional wildcard, and those *never* go into `<star>` tags. Those are more useful for "magic word" type triggers, like

```
+ [*] the machine [*]
- How do you know about the machine?
```

If you don't want everything to be converted to unicode, then you can write
```
import collections
import routeros_api.api_structure
structure = collections.defaultdict(routeros_api.api_structure.BytesField)
list_queues = api.get_resource(self.dhcp_profix, structure=structure)

```
import collections
import routeros_api.api_structure
structure = collections.defaultdict(routeros_api.api_structure.StringField)

```python
from sklearn import datasets
import treelite
from treelite.runtime import Predictor, Batch
import xgboost as xgb
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

model_path = './model.so'

# Load up dataset
boston = datasets.load_boston()
labels = pd.qcut(boston.target, [0, 0.33, 0.67, 1.0], [0, 1, 2])
dtrain = xgb.DMatrix(boston.data, label=labels, feature_names=boston.feature_names)

# Train the model
param = {
    'nthread': 1,
    'objective': 'multi:softprob',
    'eval_metric': 'mlogloss',
    'num_class': 3,
    'silent': 1,
    'max_depth': 5,
    'min_child_weight': 4,
    'eta': 0.3,
    'subsample': 1,
    'colsample_bytree': 1,
    'gamma': 0,
    'alpha': 0,
    'lambda': 1,
}
watchlist  = [(dtrain, 'eval')]
bst = xgb.train(param, dtrain, 2, watchlist)

# Build treelite model
bst_lite = treelite.Model.from_xgboost(bst)
bst_lite.export_lib(toolchain='gcc', libpath=model_path, verbose=True)
predictor = Predictor(model_path, verbose=True)

# Compare predictions
sample = boston.data[:2, :]
print 'treelite prediction: ', predictor.predict(
    Batch.from_npy2d(sample)
)
print 'xgboost  prediction: ', bst.predict(
    xgb.DMatrix(sample, feature_names=boston.feature_names)
)
```

@NikolajBjorner 
> I guess these are beginner issues and not issues with the pull request

Yes there are two issues there.

* You are not pointing CMake at the right directory. You need to give it the path to the directory containing the top level ``CMakeLists.txt`` file. This is in the root of the repository and it is the file that contains the line ``project(Z3 C CXX)``.

* It looks like you invoked CMake more than once and used a different path which is why you see the error about the CMake cache. Just delete everything in the build directory and start again.

It is important to understand that CMake is actually a "meta-build system" and not a build system itself.   When you run the ``cmake`` command it will configure and then generate the build system of your choice (e.g. UNIX makefiles, Ninja files, Visual studio...), these are referred to as "Generators" in CMake parlance. Once the build system is generated, you then use that that to build your project. You may find it easier to use the ``cmake-gui`` tool to pick the Generator you want to use and to also set various build options.

I'm going to try to write a guide on using the build system now.

It will be too brittle to bake in such assumptions either within a solver or outside.
The set of learned clauses, the state of the data-structures that maintain terms, can be expected to be updated unless you adapt a systematic copy-on-write discipline (e.g., use pure data-structures, which haven't been mainstream in SMT since ICS). Is it possible that even CVC4 and Yices will eventually show something different, but the test you used wasn't exercising all state change features?
For Z3 it will not be possible to ensure state purity after a pop. It was not a design goal. 
The ability to maintain underspecified, but deterministic (unless using timeouts)  solver behavior. is on the other hand a goal.

> I want to know whether CFGFast has the same strategy when handling the indirect jumps.

CFGFast uses indirect jump resolvers - you may take a look at [this](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/analyses/cfg/indirect_jump_resolvers/jumptable.py). It currently uses a different threshold internally. You should be able to set it up by passing argument `indirect_jump_target_limit` to `CFGFast.__init__()`.

> If so, are there any flags to distinguish the nodes and edges created by direct jump(definitely exist) from those created by indirect jump, which might not exists.

Good question. I think the answer is no at the moment, but you can manually check whether the basic block has an indirect-jump exit by checking if its address is in `cfg.indirect_jumps`. You can make a PR to add a special flag in graph edges so that we can differentiate direct and indirect jumps.

After spending some time studying why do the tests fail, I found out that many of the current angr features do strongly rely on the internal implementation of existing plugins, including the *Labels* plugin itself. This kind of strong coupling within the existing code makes it impossible (or very hard) to introduce major, yet atomic, internal changes into one particular module without touching the others, even if the external API and intended behavior is preserved.

Thus, I suggest to close this PR in favor of a new one, which will be like **"[WIP] Make KB an actual database"** on a **re/knowledge** branch. In this new PR I will move my works towards changing the implementation of the whole set of plugins, them usages and the KB itself at once, without splitting the work into the separate PRs. In this way the progress towards the actual goal will go much faster, because I won't need to keep compatibility with the old implementation of plugins, that will be changed anyway.

What do you think about it?

As far as I know:

Nodes of the statement graph (B) are of type `CodeLocation`, which identifies an instruction in `VEX`.
Edges represent a dependency between two `VEX` instructions.

Check this example DDG statment graph of a simple program:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/axt/angr-utils/master/examples/plot_ddg/simple1_ddg_stmt.png


Nodes of the data graph (A) are of type `ProgramVariable`, which is a pair of (`SimVariable`, `CodeLocation`, boolean). 

`SimVariable` can be 
- `SimRegisterVariable`,
- `SimTemporaryVariable`
- `SimMemoryVariable`, or its subclass `SimStackVariable`. 

CodeLocation is the same as above, and the third boolean parameter (called `initial`) is explained later.

Check `s_variable.py` in `simuvex` for more details on those. 

So the nodes represent a location in the program, where a given variable is read or written.  

Edges of the graph represent relations between the ProgramVariables. They have several types:
- use: using the previous definition of a variable (if its the first use of the variable, and no previous definition exists, then we indicate this through setting the initial flag to True)
- kill: overwriting the previous definition of a variable.
- mem_addr: using the variable as the address of a store/load operation
- mem_valu: using the variable as the value of a store operation

Check this example DDG data graph to get the concepts better:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/axt/angr-utils/master/examples/plot_ddg/simple1_ddg_data.png

The `TIMESTAMP` type in SQL Server has nothing to do with time. I'll refrain from posting my thoughts on that design decision...

https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms182776(v=SQL.90).aspx
> *The Transact-SQL timestamp data type is different from the timestamp data type defined in the SQL-2003 standard. The SQL-2003 timestamp data type is equivalent to the Transact-SQL datetime data type.*

> *A nonnullable timestamp column is semantically equivalent to a binary(8) column. A nullable timestamp column is semantically equivalent to a varbinary(8) column.*

It's not the bit in `_pywt.dwt_axis` that I'm concerned about, it's `wt.template.c/downcoef_axis` that will probably be confusing. That isn't going to be improved by whether or not it acts over the last axis, but depends purely on strides and offsets.

Once the checks are moved out of cython, `_dwt` becomes about 20 lines long:
- calculate output length
- allocate buffer
- call type-appropriate `_dec_a/d`
  - raise exception if they return nonzero

There is a direct path to doing the convolution and calculating the output. With `downcoef_axis`, there is a lot of checking to calculate the correct pointer offset (numpy arrays have byte-strides), allocate temporary buffers in C, etc. I had originally planned to do that in Cython, but unfortunately Cython doesn't support nD arrays with specified data types, and it becomes a horrible mess.

(Thought it might be possible using 2D arrays as the transform is only taken along 1 axis, and Cython now does OpenMP...)

I'm normally the first person to advocate cutting out code for ease of maintenance, but I think the very simple case (contiguous 1D data) is worth keeping separate from the strided nD case just because it gives such a good overview of how the process works for people who haven't seen any of the codebase before. There may or may not be a performance benefit, but it's more to have a simple example for clarity.

To check the *true* objective of the generator, you should refer to the [reward signals](https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/master/baselines/gail/adversary.py#L51). 

The variable naming might be a little bit misleading, where the `generator loss` is not the objective of the generator (policy).

@EvaSDK and all other conversation participants, the issue you are discussing is related to your proxy configuration and `django-ipware` configuration, not `axes` being broken. 

You need to, obviously, configure your reverse proxy to correctly pass / add / set headers and [configure `django-ipware` to check those headers strictly by setting the `IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER` key](https://github.com/un33k/django-ipware/blob/master/README.md#advanced-users) if you wish to implement functional IP checking for your project. There are configuration options for that. 

Any real project setup will require you to configure the reverse proxies you are using. I hope you are not blindly running your Django deployment without a correctly and thoughtfully configured reverse, at least.

The main thing to do regarding `axes` is to improve the documentation to explain these things to the end user, but please notice that *security does not come to your project by installing an arbitrary package from the Internet, even if that package claims to check this or that stuff and make your project more secure*. 

You need to actually understand what the packages you use do, why they do what they do, and whether or not you should use them. 

You also need to understand that discussing `django-ipware` package issues in `django-axes` issue tracker is not productive; if you wish to change how `django-ipware` works open an issue or PR there, or if you wish to change the `axes` functionality, open a PR here.

It turns out this adds duplicate cards to all pages, not just the first one.  In particular I see the following behavior when using the images_demo data that has 4 samples of the box_to_gaussian tag:

1. Open images dashboard
2. Change pagination limit to 2 (forcing samples 1 and 2 onto first page, 3 and 4 onto second page)
3. Close 'box_to_gaussian' category pane
4. Uncheck, then re-check the 'box_to_gaussian' run in the runs selector
5. Expand 'box_to_gaussian' category pane

The expected behavior is that it looks the same as it did after step 2, i.e. it looks the same.  However, what I see instead is that now each page has 4 cards, where the first two cards on each page are duplicate copies of the sample 1 and sample 2 cards (corresponding to the cards that were on the first page) and the second two cards are the expected ones.

Notably, the first two cards that were added appear to be "detached" in that they're unresponsive the brightness and contrast sliders.  The second two cards still respond to the sliders as expected.

If steps 3-5 are repeated, another set of duplicate cards will be added in just above the last set (which remains the "live" set).  It's possible to detect that they are added last rather than first because the new duplicates that appear seem to always be tied to whatever page was visible at the time that the category pane was closed.  E.g. if I navigate to page 2 as a step 2.5 above, and then do steps 3-5, the duplicate "dead" cards that appear are for samples 3 and 4 on both pages, rather than samples 1 and 2.

Here's an illustration of a typical result:

```
page 1
1 of 4 | 2 of 4 (1st copy)
1 of 4 | 2 of 4 (2nd copy)
3 of 4 | 4 of 4 (3rd copy after moving to page 2 before closing pane)
1 of 4 | 2 of 4 (live)

page 2
1 of 4 | 2 of 4 (1st copy)
1 of 4 | 2 of 4 (2nd copy)
3 of 4 | 4 of 4 (3rd copy after moving to page 2 before closing pane)
3 of 4 | 4 of 4 (live)
```

The only issue I had is, that debian uses pip3 for python3. When you don't have python2 installed, there is no pip. Therefore the "pip install" command in setup.py fails. A quick workaround was to create pip by symlinking it to pip3.

A found a similar issue in https://github.com/kytos/kytos/issues/465. There they fixed that by using `check_call([sys.executable, '-m', 'pip', 'install', ...)` to start pip. I changed that in your setup.py and now it works even, when only pip3 is installed.

```
diff --git a/setup.py b/setup.py
index 823713d7..70b60fc6 100644
--- a/setup.py
+++ b/setup.py
@@ -5,6 +5,7 @@ import os
 from distutils.core import Command
 from setuptools import setup, find_packages
 from setuptools.command import install
+from subprocess import check_call


 from pip.req import parse_requirements
@@ -28,7 +29,7 @@ def process_requirements(dep):
     iter_reqs = parse_requirements("requirements/{}".format(dep), session=u)

     for ir in iter_reqs:
-        os.system("pip install " + str(ir.req))
+        check_call([sys.executable, '-m', 'pip', 'install', str(ir.req)])


 def custom_command_driver(driver):
``

I'll be handling this issue, and I intend to fix most of the go related build this week for the api-compiler use cases.

I'm not immediately sure what's causing the import cycles, but they can occur if protos of the same package aren't compiled together - I didn't check if the makefile is doing that.

Manually, if you are compiling the pubsub protos:
```
protoc --go_out=plugins=grpc:. google/pubsub/v1/pubsub.proto
protoc --go_out=plugins=gprc:. google/protobuf/empty.proto google.protobuf/timestamp.proto
```

If you want a particular go package path for imports, include the `go_package` option in each of the compiled protos.
So:
```
// pubsub.proto
...
option go_package = "github.com/myorg/myrepo/pubsub/v1";

// timestamp.proto
option go_package = "github.com/myorg/myrepo/protobuf";

etc.
```

```
from __future__ import division, print_function, absolute_import

import tflearn
import tensorflow as tf
import numpy as np
from tflearn.layers.core import input_data, dropout, fully_connected
from tflearn.layers.conv import conv_2d, max_pool_2d
from tflearn.layers.normalization import local_response_normalization
from tflearn.layers.estimator import regression
tf.reset_default_graph()
tflearn.init_graph(seed=1)
tf.set_random_seed(1)
np.random.seed(1)


# Data loading and preprocessing
import tflearn.datasets.mnist as mnist
X, Y, testX, testY = mnist.load_data(one_hot=True)
X = X.reshape([-1, 28, 28, 1])
testX = testX.reshape([-1, 28, 28, 1])

# Building convolutional network
network = input_data(shape=[None, 28, 28, 1], name='input')
network = conv_2d(network, 32, 3, activation='relu', regularizer="L2")
network = max_pool_2d(network, 2)
network = local_response_normalization(network)
network = conv_2d(network, 64, 3, activation='relu', regularizer="L2")
network = max_pool_2d(network, 2)
network = local_response_normalization(network)
network = fully_connected(network, 128, activation='tanh')
network = dropout(network, 0.8)
network = fully_connected(network, 256, activation='tanh')
network = dropout(network, 0.8)
network = fully_connected(network, 10, activation='softmax')
network = regression(network, optimizer='adam', learning_rate=0.01,
                     loss='categorical_crossentropy', name='target')

# Training
model = tflearn.DNN(network, tensorboard_dir='/output', tensorboard_verbose=0)
model.fit({'input': X}, {'target': Y}, n_epoch=1,
           validation_set=({'input': testX}, {'target': testY}),
           snapshot_step=100, show_metric=True, run_id='test_3_seed_2')
```

Yeah I saw that after we talked in the hallway. As specified, none of them work for us due to one reason or another, however, it helps suggest strategies for how it could work.

Proposal that was resolve this and #3215:

At the start of error handling, set every signal handler we care about to one that simply records the signal that was raised. After calling all cleanup functions, we restore the original signal handlers and call each of them in the order they occurred.

Potential downsides of this approach:

1) A new signal will interrupt us calling the signal handlers of the deferred signals. I don't think we really care about this because this is the behavior you get by default in Python -- a new signal, including one of the same type, interrupts a signal handler.
2) We don't pass the stack frame from when the signal was first encountered to the signal handler. I'm not aware of a way to reliably do this ([getsignal](https://docs.python.org/2/library/signal.html#signal.getsignal) doesn't always return a callable and often doesn't if the signal handler is the default). Again, I'm not sure we really care and passing the current stack frame is arguably more correct.

I received some information from DYN support. Turns out, DYN has 2 different service for primary DNS, "Managed DNS" and "Standard DNS". Only records in "Standard DNS" service can be updated using the `certbot-dns-rfc2136` plugin since it supports TSIG based updated. The "Managed DNS" service does not support TSIG based updates and can only be updated by the HTTP api.
There's also a "DynDNS Pro" service that supports the TSIG based update system.

The difference between the two is that the "Managed DNS" has a much larger and more redundant network than the "Standard DNS" service.

This plugin targets the "Managed DNS" service.

No worries - I think I understand what is going wrong!
So when you run certbot it makes a directory at `/etc/letsencrypt/` which stores all of your certificate files.
You deleted that directory but your apache config file still has a refernce to it. So when certbot first tries to do anything it checks that your apache config file is valid, it then notices that it's making reference to a directory that doesn't exist and decides that it doesn't want to do anything until you have a valid apache config.
I'd delete the lines referencing your ssl certs from your apache config file and run the whole process again, especially since it sounds like you deleted your previously generated certificates.
Those config files should live at `/etc/apache2/sites-available` and will likely by symbolically linked into `/etc/apache2/sites-enabled`.
In the future if you're having weird redirect issues it's likely due to something in one of your apache configuration files rather than with your lets encrypt certificates.
Let me know if that helps!

Looks good except I don't think my comment at https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/5608#pullrequestreview-99937605 got addressed.

To expand a little more on why I think it's important, Certbot will currently exit with a zero status and no warnings if there's no matching vhosts so it's not clear it didn't do what the user asked.

While we may want to change the behavior around deploy/enhance if we fail for a domain in the future (what's being proposed is we continue execution so we work for subsequent domains), I don't think we should make any of those changes here as it requires some UI changes to make sure the domains we failed on is 100% clear and not hidden in the scrollback in the user's terminal. Currently if we fail on one, we exit with an error and rollback changes to all which is while arguably less useful, I think make things pretty clear to the user: your request to deploy/enhance the cert failed.

What is still needed for this PR, is to utilize the new mock support of the zhmcclient. Unfortunately, this will change quite a bit in this PR.

One approach would be:
- a script that creates the resource dictionary that is needed for mocking, as a JSON file, from an HMC, using the low level `Session.get()` operation. This file can lateron be used for setting up the mocking and it also defines expected results for retrieval operations. This script is similar to `create_hmcinfo.py`, just the output format is different.
- a function test that reads the resource dictionary JSON file of a particular HMC and that drives operations against that HMC, and compares the expected results. This script is similar to `run_hmcinfo.py`, except that it cannot generically rely on the operations in the file, but needs to have its own understanding of the operations. 
- a unit test that sets up zhmcclient mocking based upon a resource dictionary JSON file and that drives the function test mentioned above against the mocked environment.

Hi @bluesurfer. 

"Intended" probably not. "Expected" yes. 🙂

If you look at the `__getitem__` implementation: 

https://github.com/django-crispy-forms/django-crispy-forms/blob/e63d8934afe2fcfc677c459550f1136167497ee3/crispy_forms/helper.py#L79-L114

You'll see it first checks for the relevant attribute on the helper. We've got a `template` and it defaults to `None`: 

https://github.com/django-crispy-forms/django-crispy-forms/blob/e63d8934afe2fcfc677c459550f1136167497ee3/crispy_forms/helper.py#L215

That's what you're hitting. 

If you have similarly named fields you'll probably have to reconstruct the `LayoutSlice` (from the later logic in `__getitem__`) yourself. 

One option might be to name your form field differently (using `label` etc for appearances) and then override `clean` to convert back to the underlying field name you need, i.e. `template` here. (You'll have to experiment with that. 🙂)

Not sure, what you are doing, but the CPU version has 8 nested for-loops. A working TensorFlow implemenation is:

```python
def correlation(A, B, kernel_size, max_displacement, stride_1, stride_2, pad, data_format):
    import numpy as np
    """ This is a fallback option for the correlation cost layer
    """
    assert kernel_size == 1
    assert data_format == 'NCHW'

    b, c, h, w = A.shape.as_list()

    r = max_displacement / stride_2
    d = 2 * r + 1
    border = max_displacement
    dr = int(max_displacement / stride_2)

    Cout = int(d * d)
    Hout = int(np.ceil((h + 2 * (pad - border)) / stride_1))
    Wout = int(np.ceil((w + 2 * (pad - border)) / stride_1))

    Apad = tf.pad(A, [[0, 0], [0, 0], [pad, pad], [pad, pad]])
    Bpad = tf.pad(B, [[0, 0], [0, 0], [pad, pad], [pad, pad]])

    res = []

    for tj in range(-dr, dr + 1):
        for ti in range(-dr, dr + 1):
            res_h = []
            for h in range(0, Hout):
                h1 = int(h * stride_1 + max_displacement)
                res_w = []
                for w in range(0, Wout):
                    w1 = int(w * stride_1 + max_displacement)

                    patchA = Apad[:, :, h1:h1+1, w1:w1+1]

                    w2 = w1 + ti * stride_2
                    h2 = h1 + tj * stride_2

                    patchB = Bpad[:, :, h2:h2+1, w2:w2+1]

                    ans = tf.reduce_mean(patchA * patchB, axis=[1], keepdims=True)
                    res_w.append(ans)

                res_h.append(tf.concat(res_w, axis=3))
            res.append(tf.concat(res_h, axis=2))
    res = tf.concat(res, axis=1)
    return res
```

But this requires H and W dimension to be known beforehand (not `None`)

import trio

at_exit_entered = False

class bar:
    async def __aenter__(self):

    async def __aexit__(self, *tb):

        global at_exit_entered
        at_exit_entered = True
        await trio.sleep(0)


async def nums(seed):
    async with bar():
        for i in range(seed):
            await trio.sleep(0)
            yield i


async def iterate_nums():
    with trio.open_cancel_scope() as cs:
        async for i in nums(10):

            # cs.shield = True
            await trio.sleep(0.1)
            # cs.shield = False


async def foo():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as n:

        await trio.sleep(0.8)
        n.cancel_scope.cancel()

in `.spec` and the patch in question has "(DROP-IN-RPM)" in subject so you explicitly told `rdopkg` not to include that patch in `.spec` file.

On `rhos-8.0-rhel-7`, with the patch cherry-picked in local `rhos-8.0-patches`, `rdopkg patch -l` results in noop because there are no new patches (you filtered the new one out, output says "7 filtered out by regex" instead of previous "6" so all is as it should be).

On `rhos-9.0-rhel7`, with the patch cherry-picked in local `rhos-8.0-patches`, `rdopkg patch -l` results in a new "Updated patches" commit because the patches branch changed from the last time they were generated (probably a rebase), but the new patch is again filtered out as it should (rdopkg says "3 filtered out by regex" instead of "2" without the patch as expected).

* If you don't want to include the patch in. spec and RPM, that's exactly what's consistentnly happening.
* If you want to include the patch in .spec and RPM, you should drop (DROP-IN-RPM) from it.

Either way, I see no bug here.

So this isn't a fundamental fix of this issue, but I have a workaround that I use for this case. The part that matter for me is the function/module signature. I usually try to get

some_name_I_want.function_name(var1,var2)

independent of the folder structure anyway. One can control this behavior by updating <add_directive_header> function of Documenter class of autodoc to introduce manual control of module, so I use

.. autofunction test_data.@ClassFolder.classMethod
    :module: some_name_I_want

I made the update in autodoc itself, but one could overload the <add_directive_header> in the documenter inherited in matlabdomain as well.
